sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: The International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) publication Annex 6, titled Operation of Aircraft, contains the international Standards and Recommended Practices (SARPs) applicable to the issuing of an air operator's certificate (AOC) to conduct international commercial air transport. It has a JAA counterpart, EU-OPS, which is based on Annex 6. The ICAO document is published in three parts covering Part 1 Aeroplanes used in International Commercial Air Transport, Part 2 Aeroplanes used in General Aviation, and Part 3 Helicopters used in International Commercial Air Transport.

sections.title: Compliance with the Law
sections.content: Because Annex 6 contains the international standards which must be embodied in national law, operators of aeroplanes engaged in international operations will be subject to the laws of foreign states as well as the laws of the State of the Operator (and the State of Registration of the aeroplane if different). It is incumbent upon the Operator to ensure that all employees, wherever they are around the world, comply with the laws of the state in which they are operating or based. Specifically, pilots are to be conversant with the rules of the air and ATS regulations for the airspace in which they will be flying.

sections.title: Operational Control
sections.content: The Operator or a designated representative will have responsibility for operational control (exercising authority over the initiation, continuation, diversion or termination of a flight). The responsibility may only be delegated to the commander (PIC), and exceptionally to the flight operations officer or dispatcher if the approved method of supervision of flight operations requires the use of flight operations/dispatch personnel.

sections.title: Safety
sections.content: The stated aim of ICAO is to encourage safe and efficient development and growth of international commercial aviation. To this end the certification of Operators is one area where regulation and application of auditable standards can help achieve the aim. Before an Operator is granted an air operator's certificate (AOC), the necessary approval to conduct commercial (revenue earning) operations, the Operator must demonstrate that the operation is not only commercially (financially) viable, but also safe.

sections.title: Alternate Aerodromes
sections.content: An alternate aerodrome is defined as an aerodrome to which an aircraft may proceed when it becomes either impossible or inadvisable to proceed to, or to land at, the aerodrome of intended landing. Alternate aerodromes include: Take-off alternate - an aerodrome at which an aircraft can land should this become necessary shortly after take-off; En route alternate - an aerodrome at which an aircraft would be able to land after experiencing an abnormal or emergency condition whilst en route; ETOPS en route alternate - a suitable and appropriate aerodrome at which an aeroplane would be able to land after experiencing an engine shut down or other abnormal or emergency condition whilst en route in an ETOPS operation; Destination alternate - an aerodrome to which an aircraft may proceed, should it become either impossible or inadvisable to land at the aerodrome of intended landing.

sections.title: Flight time
sections.content: Flight time means the total time from the moment an aeroplane first moves for the purpose of taking off until the moment it comes to rest after landing, or in the case of helicopters, from the moment the rotor blades start turning until the moment the rotor blades stop after landing.

sections.title: Flight Safety and Accident Prevention
sections.content: The operator shall ensure that all operations are conducted in accordance with the operator's operations manual and that the aeroplane is airworthy. The operator shall also ensure that passengers are briefed on safety matters and that all crew members are competent and aware of their duties and responsibilities.

sections.title: Maintenance Release
sections.content: A maintenance release is a document which certifies that maintenance work has been completed satisfactorily and that the aeroplane is airworthy for flight. It must be signed by an appropriately authorized person.

sections.title: Lighting of Aircraft
sections.content: All aircraft operated at night must display navigation lights. The navigation lights must be displayed from sunset to sunrise and at such other times when light conditions make it difficult to see the aircraft at a distance.

sections.title: Navigation Lights
sections.content: Navigation lights shall consist of a red light on the left wing tip, a green light on the right wing tip, and a white light on the tail. These lights help other aircraft determine the direction of travel of the aircraft at night.

sections.title: Navigation Light Angles
sections.content: Navigation lights are positioned on the aeroplane to indicate to another air user the direction that the aeroplane is heading. Port (left) red and starboard (right) green lights show horizontally through an angle of 110 degrees either side of dead ahead. The tail white light shows through an angle of 70 degrees either side of the plane of symmetry (140 degrees total) of the aeroplane. Vertically, the port and starboard lights show through 180 degrees, and the tail light is visible through 360 degrees.

sections.title: Lighting Scheme
sections.content: The majority of aeroplanes now flying have navigation lights that show a steady light at all times. Older aircraft may have lights which alternate on/off together, or alternate on/off independently. Anti-collision lights may be of the rotating beacon type or the high intensity strobe discharge type (Falcon beacon).

sections.title: Introduction to EU-OPS
sections.content: EU-OPS is the European Civil Aviation Conference (ECAC) equivalent of ICAO Annex 6, Part 1. It contains the operational requirements for commercial air transport operations by aeroplanes within European member states. EU-OPS is divided into several parts covering General requirements, Aeroplane requirements, and Crew requirements.

sections.title: Operator Responsibilities
sections.content: The operator is responsible for the safe operation of the aeroplane. This includes ensuring that all crew members are properly trained and qualified, that the aeroplane is properly maintained, and that all operational procedures are followed.

sections.title: Operations Manual
sections.content: The operator must have an operations manual (OM) containing all the information necessary for the safe conduct of flight operations. The OM must include operational procedures, checklists, training requirements, and emergency procedures.

sections.title: Crew Requirements
sections.content: The operator must ensure that all crew members are properly licensed and qualified. This includes medical certificates, type ratings, and recurrent training requirements.

sections.title: Flight Time Limitations
sections.content: EU-OPS sets specific limitations on flight time and duty time for crew members to prevent fatigue. These include maximum flight time per month, per year, and mandatory rest periods.

sections.title: Quality System
sections.content: The Operator is required to establish and maintain a Quality System to ensure safe operational practices and airworthy aeroplanes. A Quality Manager is to be appointed to ensure compliance with the quality system. Compliance monitoring must include a feed-back system to the Accountable Manager who has responsibility to the Authority for compliance with the requirements of the AOC.

sections.title: Accident Prevention and Flight Safety Programme
sections.content: Each Operator is required to establish and maintain an Accident Prevention and Flight Safety programme which may be integrated with the Quality System. The programme is to be administered by a person appointed by the Operator. Such a programme is to include: risk awareness, occurrence reporting, incident and accident evaluation, and flight data monitoring.

sections.title: Additional Crew Members
sections.content: Where additional crew members (e.g. personnel engaged in checking, non-operating crew, or where animals are carried, veterinarian personnel) are carried on operational flights, the Operator is to ensure that such personnel are properly trained and proficient in the execution of their duty. Passengers must be able to distinguish between cabin crew and additional crew.

sections.title: Ditching
sections.content: Ditching, or the deliberate alighting of an aeroplane on the surface of the sea, is a possibility where aircraft are operated over the sea at a distance where in the event of engine failure the aircraft cannot reach land suitable for an emergency landing. To minimize the risk from ditching, operators are not to operate aeroplanes with a passenger seating capacity more than 30 at a distance from land greater than 120 minutes flying time at cruising speed, or 400 NM whichever is less, unless the aeroplane complies with the requirements of the applicable airworthiness code.

sections.title: Carriage of Persons
sections.content: Operators are to ensure that persons (crew and passengers) are only carried in parts of aeroplanes which are designed for the accommodation of persons. The Commander may grant temporary access to other parts of the aeroplane for the purpose of taking action for the safety of the aeroplane, or persons, animals or goods which are in such areas providing the area is that to which designed access is possible.

sections.title: Crew Responsibilities
sections.content: Crew members are responsible for the discharge of their duties in respect of the safety of the aeroplane and its occupants. The discharge of such duties is to be in accordance with the procedures laid down in the Operations Manual (OM) including where necessary, use of the Operator's occurrence reporting system. Any fault or malfunction including an emergency situation which could affect the airworthiness or safe operation of the aeroplane is to be reported to the Commander. Any incident that endangered or could have endangered the safety of operations is to be reported to the Commander.

sections.title: Crew Fitness
sections.content: A crew member is not to present himself or herself for duty on an aeroplane: if under the influence of any drug that may affect his/her faculties in a manner contrary to safety; until a reasonable period of time has elapsed after deep water diving; until a reasonable period of time has elapsed after blood donation; if he or she is in any doubt as to his or her ability to accomplish the assigned duty; or if he or she knows or suspects that he or she is suffering from fatigue to the extent where safety may be endangered.

sections.title: Alcohol and Drug Rules
sections.content: A crew member must not: consume alcohol less than 8 hours prior to reporting for duty or the commencement of standby duty; report for duty with a blood alcohol level exceeding 0.2 promille; or consume alcohol during flight time or when on standby.

sections.title: Commander Responsibilities
sections.content: The Commander is responsible for the safety of all crew members, passengers and cargo on board as soon as he/she arrives on board, until he/she leaves the aeroplane at the end of the flight. Additionally he/she: is responsible for the operation and safety of the aeroplane from the moment the aeroplane is first ready to move for taxiing for take-off until it finally comes to rest at the end of the flight and the primary propulsion units (engines excluding APU) are shut down; has the authority to give all commands deemed necessary for the purpose of ensuring the safety of the aeroplane and the persons and property carried; has the authority to disembark (off load) any person, or part of cargo, which in his/her opinion presents a potential hazard to the safety of the aeroplane.

sections.title: Commander Authority
sections.content: The Commander will not allow any person to be carried in the aeroplane who appears to be under the influence of alcohol or drugs and may endanger the safety of the aeroplane of the occupants. May refuse to carry any inadmissible passenger, deportee or person in custody (potentially disruptive passenger) if the carriage poses a safety risk. Is to ensure that all passengers are briefed as to the location of emergency exits, and the location and the operation of safety equipment. Is to ensure that all operational procedures and checklists are complied with in accordance with the requirements of the OM.

sections.title: Authority of the Commander
sections.content: The Commander is the representative of the State of Registration/State of the Operator from the time he/she enters the aeroplane until the time he/she leaves the aeroplane. The Operator is required to take all reasonable measures to ensure that all persons carried on board the aeroplane obey the lawful commands of the Commander given for the purpose of securing the safety of the aeroplane and all the persons on board.

sections.title: Admission to Flight Deck
sections.content: The only personnel permitted to enter the flight deck are: the Operating Crew; a representative of the Authority in the performance of his/her official duty; persons permitted to do so in accordance with instruction in the OM. Note: The final decision concerning admittance to the flight deck rests with the Commander.

sections.title: Unauthorized Carriage
sections.content: The Operator is to ensure that no persons hides him or herself (stowaways), or hides any cargo on board an aeroplane.

sections.title: Portable Electronic Devices
sections.content: The Operator is to take all reasonable measures to prevent the use of any portable electronic device on board an aeroplane that could adversely affect the performance of the aeroplane's systems and equipment.

sections.title: Drugs and Alcohol
sections.content: Where the safety of the aeroplane or persons on board would be affected, the Operator is to ensure that no person is permitted to enter an aeroplane if it is obvious that the person is under the influence of drugs or alcohol.

sections.title: Endangering Safety
sections.content: The Operator is to take all reasonable measures to ensure that no person acts negligently or in such a manner that endangers the aeroplane or persons aboard, or causes an aeroplane to endanger any person or property.

sections.title: Documents to Be Carried
sections.content: The following documents are required to be carried on board each flight: The Certificate of Registration (original); The certificate of airworthiness (original); The Noise Certificate (original); The air operators certificate (copy will suffice); The Aircraft radio licence (original); Third party insurance certificate (copy will suffice). Each flight crew member is to carry the valid flight crew licence plus any necessary ratings.

sections.title: Manuals to Be Carried
sections.content: The Operator is to ensure that the following manuals are carried on all flights: Parts of the OM relating to flight crew duty; Parts of the OM required for the conduct of the flight; The current Aeroplane Flight Manual (AFM) (unless the Authority has approved the OM as containing the necessary information).

sections.title: Additional Information and Forms to Be Carried
sections.content: The Operator is to ensure that, where relevant, the following are to be carried on all flights: The Operational Flight Plan (OFP); The required parts of the Aeroplane Technical Log; The ATS flight plan; Appropriate NOTAM/AIS briefing material; Appropriate meteorological information; Mass and Balance documentation; Details of special categories of passengers; Special load notification (including dangerous cargo); Current maps and charts; Any other documentation that may be required by a state; Forms to comply with the reporting requirements of the Authority and the Operator.

sections.title: Information to Be Retained on the Ground
sections.content: The Operator is to ensure that for at least the duration of each flight (or series of flights): Information relevant to the flight and appropriate to the type of operation is preserved on the ground; and The information is retained until it can be duplicated and stored in accordance with the relevant regulations, or if this is not practicable, the information is carried in a fireproof container in the aeroplane.

sections.title: Power to Inspect
sections.content: Any person authorized by the Authority is permitted to board and fly in an aeroplane operated in accordance with the AOC (and enter and remain on the flight deck - Commander's discretion applies).

sections.title: Production and Preservation of Documents and Recordings
sections.content: Persons authorized by the Authority are permitted access to any documents and records relating to flight operations. The Operator is required to produce all such documents and records within a reasonable time when requested to do so by the Authority. Likewise, the Commander is required to produce any of the documents required to be carried on board an aeroplane when requested to do so by an authorized person. Original documentation is to be kept for the required retention period by the operator, even if during that period he ceases to be the Operator of the aeroplane. Following an incident, all FDR and CVR recordings are to be retained for a period of 60 days unless a longer period is requested by the Authority.

sections.title: Leasing of Aeroplanes
sections.content: Leasing in aviation law is the situation whereby an aeroplane is used by one operator whilst the ownership title remains with another operator. The operator using the aeroplane pays the owner an agreed sum for the use of the aeroplane over a specified period.

sections.title: Dry Lease
sections.content: Dry Lease is when the leased aeroplane is operated under the AOC of the lessee (the operator borrowing the aeroplane).

sections.title: Wet Lease
sections.content: Wet Lease is when the leased aeroplane is operated under the AOC of the lessor (the operator lending the aeroplane to the lessee).

sections.title: Weather Minima
sections.content: The operator must establish weather minima for each type of operation. These minima must not be less than those specified in EU-OPS or by the state of the aerodrome.

sections.title: Fuel Requirements
sections.content: The operator must ensure that sufficient fuel is carried for the planned flight plus contingency fuel. The minimum fuel required depends on the type of operation and the distance to the destination and alternate aerodromes.

sections.title: Alternate Aerodromes
sections.content: A take-off alternate is required when the weather conditions at the departure aerodrome are below certain minima. A destination alternate is required for IFR flights when the weather conditions at the destination are below certain minima.

sections.title: Air Operator's Certificate (AOC)
sections.content: An Air Operator's Certificate is required for commercial air transport operations. The certificate is issued by the national aviation authority (NAA) of the state in which the operator is based. The AOC specifies the types of operations that the operator is authorized to conduct.

sections.title: Operator Supervision
sections.content: The operator must have a quality system in place to ensure compliance with all applicable regulations. This includes an inspection department, a safety management system, and a training organization.

sections.title: Certification Requirements
sections.content: Before an AOC is issued, the operator must demonstrate that it has the financial viability, organizational structure, and operational capability to conduct safe operations.

sections.title: Operations Specifications
sections.content: The AOC is supported by operations specifications (Ops Specs) that specify the authorized routes, aerodromes, types of aircraft, and specific operational procedures.

sections.title: Quality System
sections.content: The operator must have a quality system that ensures compliance with all applicable requirements. The quality system must include procedures for auditing, reporting, and correcting non-compliance.

sections.title: Accountable Manager
sections.content: The operator must nominate an Accountable Manager who has corporate authority and responsibility for ensuring that all operational requirements are met. The Accountable Manager must be approved by the NAA.

sections.title: Key Post Holders
sections.content: Other post holders appointed by the operator, required and acceptable to the Authority are: Flight Operations Manager; Maintenance System Manager; Crew Training Manager; Ground Operations Manager. A single person may hold one or more of the above posts (if acceptable to the Authority), however for operators with 21 or more full time employees a minimum of two persons are required to fill the four positions.

sections.title: Main Operating Base
sections.content: The Authority will require full operational support facilities to be established and maintained at the main operating base, appropriate to the area and type of operations conducted.

sections.title: Aeroplane Maintenance
sections.content: The operator must ensure that the aeroplanes used in the operation are airworthy and are maintained in accordance with the manufacturers (approved) maintenance programme. If the operator elects to carry out the maintenance 'in-house', the operator must be approved in accordance with EASA Part 145 for the approval of maintenance operations.

sections.title: Operations Manual Part A
sections.content: Part A - General and Basic Information includes procedures and information relating to: Administration and Control of OM; Organization and Responsibilities; Operational Control and Supervision; Quality System; Crew Composition; Qualification Requirements; Crew Health Precautions; Flight Time Limitations; Operating Procedures; Dangerous Goods and Weapons; Security; Handling, Notifying and Reporting of Occurrences; Rules of the Air; Leasing.

sections.title: Operations Manual Part B
sections.content: Part B - Aeroplane Operating Matters includes procedures and information relating to: General Information and Units of Measurement; Limitations; Normal Procedures; Abnormal and Emergency Procedures; Performance; Flight Planning; Mass and Balance; Loading; Configuration Deviation List (CDL); Minimum Equipment List (MEL); Survival and Emergency Equipment including Oxygen; Emergency Evacuation Procedures; Aeroplane Systems.

sections.title: Operations Manual Part C
sections.content: Part C - Route and Aerodrome Instructions and Information is the section in which staff will find aerodrome operating minima and any special instruction for the calculation of minima and special data relating to aerodromes used as part of the operation.

sections.title: Operations Manual Part D
sections.content: Part D - Training. All personnel are required to be competent to carry out their duties. This section details the training method and requirements to ensure that all personnel are competent.

sections.title: General Rules for Operations Manuals
sections.content: The operator is responsible for ensuring that the OM: Contains all instructions and information necessary for operations personnel to conduct their duty; Contents, including all amendments or revisions, do not contravene any part of the AOC or any applicable regulation; Must be approved by the Authority; Must be prepared in the English language; Can be issued in separate volumes; Is available for all operations personnel who must have easy access; Must be amended and kept up to date.

sections.title: Competence of Operations Personnel
sections.content: An operator shall ensure that all personnel assigned to, or directly involved in, ground and flight operations are properly instructed, have demonstrated their abilities in their particular duties and are aware of their responsibilities and the relationship of such duties to the operation as a whole.

sections.title: Adequate Aerodrome
sections.content: An adequate aerodrome is an aerodrome which the operator considers to be satisfactory, taking account of the applicable performance requirements and runway characteristics; at the expected time of use, the aerodrome will be available and equipped with necessary ancillary services such as ATS, sufficient lighting, communications, weather reporting, navaids and emergency services.

sections.title: ETOPS
sections.content: ETOPS (Extended range operations for two-engine aeroplanes) are operations with two-engine aeroplanes approved by the Authority (ETOPS approval), to operate beyond the threshold distance determined in accordance with EU-OPS from an adequate aerodrome.

sections.title: Adequate ETOPS En Route Alternate
sections.content: An adequate ETOPS en route alternate aerodrome is an adequate aerodrome which additionally, at the expected time of use, has an ATS facility and at least one instrument approach procedure.

sections.title: En Route Alternate
sections.content: An en route alternate (ERA) aerodrome is an adequate aerodrome along the route which may be required at the planning stage.

sections.title: 3% ERA
sections.content: A 3% ERA is an en route alternate aerodrome selected for the purposes of reducing contingency fuel to 3%.

sections.title: Isolated Aerodrome
sections.content: If acceptable to the Authority, the destination aerodrome can be considered as an isolated aerodrome if the fuel required (diversion plus final) to the nearest adequate destination alternate aerodrome is more than: For aeroplanes with reciprocating engines, fuel to fly for 45 minutes plus 15% of the flight time planned to be spent at cruising level or two hours, whichever is less; For aeroplanes with turbine engines, fuel to fly for two hours at normal cruise consumption above the destination aerodrome, including final reserve fuel.

sections.title: Equivalent Position
sections.content: An equivalent position is a position that can be established by means of a DME distance, a suitably located NDB or VOR, SRE or PAR fix or any other suitable fix between three and five miles from threshold that independently establishes the position of the aeroplane.

sections.title: Critical Phases of Flight
sections.content: Critical phases of flight are the take-off run, the take-off flight path, the final approach, the landing, including the landing roll, and any other phases of flight at the discretion of the commander.

sections.title: Contingency Fuel
sections.content: Contingency fuel is the fuel required to compensate for unforeseen factors which could have an influence on the fuel consumption to the destination aerodrome such as deviations of an individual aeroplane from the expected fuel consumption data, deviations from forecast meteorological conditions and deviations from planned routings and/or cruising levels/altitudes.

sections.title: Separate Runways
sections.content: Separate runways are runways at the same aerodrome that are separate landing surfaces. These runways may overlay or cross in such a way that if one of the runways is blocked, it will not prevent the planned type of operations on the other runway. Each runway shall have a separate approach procedure based on a separate navigation aid.

sections.title: Approved One-Engine-Inoperative Cruise Speed
sections.content: For ETOPS, the approved one-engine-inoperative cruise speed for the intended area of operation shall be a speed, within the certified limits of the aeroplane, selected by the operator and approved by the regulatory Authority.

sections.title: ETOPS Area
sections.content: An ETOPS area is an area containing airspace within which an ETOPS approved aeroplane remains in excess of the specified flying time in still air (in standard conditions) at the approved one-engine-inoperative cruise speed from an adequate ETOPS route alternate aerodrome.

sections.title: RVSM
sections.content: RVSM (reduced vertical separation minima) is now the generally accepted method of applying vertical separation between aircraft operating between FL290 and FL410. Before an operator can operate over routes at flight levels where RVSM is applied, approval must be granted by the Authority and such approval noted in the AOC. Basically, the crew must be trained in RVSM and the aircraft equipped with A/TCAS, an altitude reporting SSR system, and have an autopilot 'height lock' with an altitude deviation alerting system (audio or visual).

sections.title: Take-off Alternate Planning Minima
sections.content: For an aerodrome to be selected as a take-off alternate, the weather reports/forecasts must indicate that during the period 1 hour before until 1 hour after the ETA at the (alternate) aerodrome the met conditions will be at or above the applicable aerodrome operating minima. In this respect, the ceiling must be taken into account when non-precision or circling approaches are the only available instrument approach option. Additionally, any limitations related to one engine inoperative must also be considered.

sections.title: Two Engine Aeroplane Take-off Alternate
sections.content: The take-off alternate must be located within either one hour flight time at the one-engine-inoperative cruise speed or, where approved, the operator's ETOPS diversion time, subject to any MEL restriction, up to a maximum of 2 hours at the one-engine-inoperative cruise speed.

sections.title: Three or More Engine Take-off Alternate
sections.content: For three or more engine aeroplanes, two hours flight time at the one-engine-inoperative cruise speed specified in the AFM in still air standard conditions based on the actual take-off mass.

sections.title: Destination Planning Minima
sections.content: For an aerodrome to be selected as a destination, the weather reports/forecasts must indicate that during the period 1 hour before until 1 hour after the ETA at the aerodrome, the met conditions will be at or above: The RVR/visibility must be as required for the aerodrome operating minima; For a non-precision or circling approach the ceiling must be at or above MDH; or Two destination alternates must be selected.

sections.title: Destination Alternate Rules
sections.content: At least one destination alternate (diversion) aerodrome must be selected for each IFR flight unless: Both the duration of the planned flight from take-off to landing does not exceed 6 hours, and two separate runways are available and useable at the destination aerodrome and the met reports/forecasts indicate that from 1 hour before until 1 hour after ETA at the destination, the ceiling will be at least 2000 ft or circling height +500 ft (whichever is greater) and the visibility will be not less than 5 km; or The destination is so isolated that no useable diversion aerodrome exists.

sections.title: ETOPS En Route Alternate Planning Minima
sections.content: An operator shall only select an aerodrome as an ETOPS en route alternate aerodrome when the appropriate weather reports or forecasts indicate that, between the anticipated time of landing until one hour after the latest possible time of landing, conditions calculated by adding the additional limits will exist. For precision approach procedure: Authorized DH/DA plus an increment of 200 ft and authorized visibility plus an increment of 800 metres. For non-precision approach or circling approach: Authorized MDH/MDA plus an increment of 400 ft and authorized visibility plus an increment of 1500 metres.

sections.title: Meteorological Conditions Requirements
sections.content: For an IFR flight, the commander shall only commence take-off or continue beyond the point from which a revised flight plan applies in the event of in-flight re-planning, when information is available indicating that the expected weather conditions at ETA at the destination and/or required alternate aerodromes, are at or above the planning minima. On an IFR flight, the commander shall only continue towards the planned destination aerodrome when the latest information available indicates that at ETA the weather conditions at the destination or at least one destination alternate are at or above the applicable aerodrome operating minima.

sections.title: Approach and Landing Conditions
sections.content: Before commencing any approach to land, the commander is to be satisfied that according to the information available, the weather at the aerodrome and the condition of the runway to be used will not prevent a safe approach, landing or missed approach being made in accordance with the performance information in the OM.

sections.title: Aerodrome Operating Minima
sections.content: For all aerodromes (departure, destination or alternate), and for instrument approaches and runways used within the context of the operation and approved for use by the operator, the operator is required to define the applicable aerodrome operating minima. This will be: For take-off, the minimum acceptable met visibility or, where available, the minimum acceptable RVR; For instrument approaches, the AOM consists of DA/H or MDA/H and the minimum applicable met visibility or RVR.

sections.title: Commencement and Continuation of Approach
sections.content: The commander or the pilot to whom conduct of the flight has been delegated may commence an instrument approach regardless of the reported RVR/visibility but the approach shall not be continued beyond the outer marker (or equivalent position) on a precision approach, or below 1000 ft on a non-precision approach, if the reported RVR/visibility is less than the applicable minima. Where RVR is not available, RVR values may be derived by converting the reported visibility: With HI approach and runway lighting, multiply by 1.5 day or 2.0 night; With any other type of lighting, multiply by 1.0 day or 1.5 night; No lighting, multiply by 1.0 day.

sections.title: Decision Point During Approach
sections.content: If after passing the outer marker or equivalent position on a precision approach, the reported RVR/visibility falls below the applicable minimum, the approach may be continued to DA/H or MDA/H. Where no outer marker or equivalent position exists, the commander shall make the decision to continue or abandon the approach before descending below 1000 ft above the aerodrome on the final approach segment. The approach may be continued below DA/H or MDA/H and the landing may be completed provided that the required visual reference is established at the DA/H or MDA/H and is maintained.

sections.title: RVR Controlling Values
sections.content: The touch-down zone RVR is always controlling. If reported and relevant, the midpoint and stop-end RVR are also controlling. The minimum RVR value for the midpoint is 125 m or the RVR required for the touch-down zone if less, and 75 m for the stop-end. For aeroplanes equipped with a roll-out guidance or control system, the minimum RVR value for the midpoint is 75 m. 'Relevant' means that part of the runway used during the high speed phase of the landing down to a speed of approximately 60 knots.

sections.title: Instrument Departure and Approach Procedures
sections.content: Where Standard Instrument Departures (SIDs) and Standard Arrival Procedures (STARs) have been established by the state for aerodromes used in the operation, the defined procedures are to be used. Any procedure published may be modified by specific ATC instruction and the commander may accept alternative instructions with the provision that the appropriate obstacle clearance criteria are met. The final approach must be flown visually or in accordance with the established instrument procedure.

sections.title: Noise Abatement Procedures
sections.content: Noise abatement is a major consideration of all instrument departures and arrivals. Modern aircraft are much more quiet than older aeroplanes but the legacy of noise reduction through procedures is incorporated in the design of SIDs and approach procedures. The responsibility for the establishment of the specific procedures rests with the operator. A critical factor to be considered in Noise Abatement Procedures is the actual take-off mass of the aircraft.

sections.title: Routes and Areas of Operation
sections.content: The operator is to make sure that any restrictions or limitations imposed by the Authority with regard to routes and areas of operation are complied with. For the routes and areas of operation, the operator is to ensure that: Ground facilities and services (including meteorological services) are provided and are adequate; The performance of the aeroplanes used ensures compliance with the minimum flight altitude requirements; The equipment fitted meets the minimum requirements; Appropriate maps and charts are available; Where twin-engine aeroplanes are used, adequate aerodromes are available within the defined time/distance limitations.

sections.title: Operations in MNPS Airspace
sections.content: Certain specified areas of airspace are designated as MNPS (minimum navigational performance specification) areas where special navigational requirements exist. Due to the lack of ground based radio navigation aids and the high level of traffic density, standards for determination of position and track need to be specified. The Authority will give approval (in the AOC) for operators to conduct operations in MNPS airspace. For operations in MNPSA the operator must ensure that contingency procedures specified by the Authority are included in the OM.

sections.title: Operating Limitations
sections.content: All weather operations are divided into categories based on the visibility and cloud ceiling. These include Visual Flight Rules (VFR), Special VFR, Instrument Flight Rules (IFR), and specific approach categories.

sections.title: Category I Approach
sections.content: A Category I approach is a precision approach using ILS, MLS, or PAR with a DH not below 200 ft and RVR not less than 550 m.

sections.title: Category II Approach
sections.content: A Category II approach is a precision approach with a DH not below 100 ft and RVR not less than 300 m.

sections.title: Category III Approach
sections.content: Category III approaches include IIIA (DH below 100 ft or no DH, RVR not less than 175 m), IIIB (RVR not less than 50 m), and IIIC (no DH, no RVR limitation).

sections.title: Low Visibility Procedures
sections.content: Low visibility procedures (LVP) are required when RVR is below 400 m. These procedures include specific taxiway lighting, runway protection, and air traffic control procedures.

sections.title: Runway Visual Range (RVR)
sections.content: RVR is the distance over which a pilot can see runway markings or lights from the cockpit. It is the primary factor in determining approach and landing minimums.

sections.title: Decision Height (DH)
sections.content: DH is the height at which a decision must be made to continue the approach or to execute a missed approach. Below DH, the approach must not continue unless the required visual reference is established.

sections.title: Equipment Requirements
sections.content: All weather operations require specific equipment including radio altimeters, ILS receivers, and in some cases, enhanced vision systems.

sections.title: Classification of Aeroplanes
sections.content: EU-OPS classifies aeroplanes by performance: Class A - Aeroplanes with a maximum take-off mass exceeding 5700 kg or with a maximum certified seating configuration of more than 9 passengers; Class B - All other aeroplanes; Class C - Aeroplanes powered by turbo jet engines or having a maximum take-off mass not exceeding 5700 kg and a maximum certified seating configuration of not more than 9 passengers.

sections.title: Low Visibility Operations
sections.content: Low Visibility Operations are conducted when RVR is below 400 m. These operations require specific procedures for taxiing, approach, landing and rollout. Low visibility procedures include specific taxiway lighting, runway protection, and air traffic control procedures. All ground movements must be conducted in accordance with the LVP.

sections.title: Non-precision Approach
sections.content: A non-precision approach is an instrument approach which does not provide lateral guidance. The pilot must establish a Minimum Descent Height (MDH) and not descend below this height unless visual reference is established. The visual reference must include elements of the approach lighting system, runway markings, or runway lights.

sections.title: Minimum Descent Height
sections.content: Minimum Descent Height (MDH) is the lowest height to which a pilot may descend during a non-precision approach without visual reference. Below MDH, the approach must not be continued unless the required visual reference is established and maintained.

sections.title: Visual Reference Requirements
sections.content: For a landing to be completed, the required visual reference must be established at the DH/MDH and maintained. The visual reference includes: Elements of the approach lighting system, runway markings, runway lights, or threshold lights. The pilot must be able to see the runway environment and assess the aircraft position and drift.

sections.title: Precision Approach - Category I
sections.content: Category I operations require: DH not below 200 ft; RVR not less than 550 m; Runway visual aids as specified; Aircraft and crew appropriately qualified; Ground facilities serviceable.

sections.title: Precision Approach - Category II
sections.content: Category II operations require: DH not below 100 ft; RVR not less than 300 m; Both runway centreline lights and touch-down zone lights required; Aircraft with appropriate auto-land capability; Specialized training required.

sections.title: Precision Approach - Category III
sections.content: Category III operations include: IIIA - DH below 100 ft or no DH, RVR not less than 175 m; IIIB - RVR not less than 50 m; IIIC - No DH, no RVR limitation. Category III operations require either fail-passive or fail-operational aircraft systems.

sections.title: No Decision Height Operations
sections.content: Category IIIB and IIIC operations do not require a DH. The pilot continues to land provided the required visual reference is available at the RVR minimum. For Category IIIB operations with fail-passive autoland, an RVR minimum of 75 m applies with roll-out guidance.

sections.title: Circling Approach
sections.content: Circling is a visual maneuver conducted after a non-precision approach to align with the runway for landing from a direction other than the final approach. Minimum circling altitude is the higher of the circling height or 1000 ft above aerodrome elevation.

sections.title: Visual Approach
sections.content: A visual approach is one conducted under VMC or where the pilot requests and maintains visual contact with the runway. ATC may clear an aircraft for a visual approach when traffic permits and the pilot can maintain visual reference.

sections.title: VFR Operating Minima
sections.content: VFR flights require: 5 km visibility (or 1500 m by day in controlled airspace); 500 ft below cloud; Clear of cloud. Specific minima may be higher for certain airspace classes.

sections.title: Special VFR
sections.content: Special VFR operations may be authorized when VMC cannot be met. Requires: ATC clearance; 1 km visibility or RVR 1800 m; Clear of cloud. Special VFR is limited to fixed wing aircraft in clear atmospheric conditions.

sections.title: Pre-flight Procedures
sections.content: Before each flight, the commander must ensure that the aeroplane is airworthy and that all required equipment is serviceable. This includes checking weather conditions, fuel requirements, and NOTAMs.

sections.title: Flight Planning
sections.content: The operator must ensure that flight planning includes consideration of weather, fuel, alternate aerodromes, and minimum safe altitudes. Navigation planning must include contingency procedures.

sections.title: Standard Instrument Departures (SIDs)
sections.content: SIDs are established procedures for departing an aerodrome under instrument flight rules. They provide obstacle clearance and noise abatement requirements.

sections.title: Standard Instrument Arrivals (STARs)
sections.content: STARs are established procedures for arriving at an aerodrome under instrument flight rules. They provide for transition from the en-route phase to the approach phase.

sections.title: Approach Procedures
sections.content: The commander must ensure that the appropriate approach procedure is selected based on the operational category of the aeroplane, weather conditions, and navigation aids available.

sections.title: Special Categories of Passengers
sections.content: Special categories of passengers include unaccompanied children, persons with reduced mobility, and VIPs. Specific procedures apply to each category.

sections.title: Stowage of Baggage and Cargo
sections.content: All baggage and cargo must be properly secured to prevent movement during flight. Baggage must be stowed in approved compartments or lockers.

sections.title: Ground Proximity Warning System (GPWS)
sections.content: GPWS provides warnings of terrain contact and is required for all commercial transport aircraft. The system provides alerts for excessive sink rate, terrain closure, and incorrect glide slope.

sections.title: Maximum Distance from Adequate Aerodrome
sections.content: Without ETOPS approval, an operator shall not operate a two-engine aeroplane over a route which contains a point further from an adequate aerodrome (under standard conditions in still air) than: For Performance Class A aeroplanes with either a maximum approved passenger seating configuration of 20 or more, or a maximum take-off mass of 45 360 kg, the distance flown in 60 minutes at the one-engine-inoperative cruise speed; For Performance Class A aeroplanes with a maximum approved passenger seating configuration of 19 or less and a maximum take-off mass less than 45 360 kg, the distance flown in 120 minutes or if approved by the Authority up to 180 minutes for turbojet aeroplanes at the one-engine-inoperative cruise speed; For Performance Class B or C aeroplanes, the distance flown in 120 minutes at the one-engine-inoperative cruise speed or 300 nautical miles, whichever is less.

sections.title: Extended Range Operations (ETOPS)
sections.content: Before an operator can conduct flights beyond the threshold distances stated for non-ETOPS operations, approval must be obtained from the Authority. This will be annotated on the AOC. It is also a requirement that the operator must maintain a reliability programme to monitor the equipment fitted to the aeroplane that is critical for ETOPS operations. Before each ETOPS flight is conducted, the operator is to ensure that a suitable en route alternate aerodrome is available within either the approved diversion time, or a diversion time based on the MEL generated serviceability status of the aeroplane, whichever is shorter.

sections.title: Minimum Flight Altitudes
sections.content: The operator is required to establish minimum flight altitudes for all route segments to be flown which require terrain clearance. The altitudes and the method of calculating must take into account the performance requirements of EU-OPS. The method of calculating the altitudes is to be approved by the Authority. If the minimum altitude specified by a state is higher than that specified by the operator, the higher altitude is to be used. When establishing the minimum flight altitude, the operator is to take into account: The position accuracy of the aeroplane; Inaccuracies in the operation of altimeters; The characteristics of the underlying terrain; The probability of encountering adverse weather; and Inaccuracies in aeronautical charts.

sections.title: Fuel Policy
sections.content: The operator is required to establish a fuel policy for flight planning and re-planning purposes to ensure that every flight carries sufficient fuel for the planned operation and reserves to cover deviations from the planned operation. A commander shall only commence a flight or continue a flight in the event of in-flight re-planning, if satisfied that the aeroplane carries at least the planned amount of useable fuel (and oil) to complete the flight safely.

sections.title: Fuel Reserve Requirements
sections.content: The pre-flight calculation of fuel required is to include taxi fuel, trip fuel, and reserve fuel consisting of: Contingency fuel; Alternate fuel; Final reserve fuel; Additional fuel if required by the type of operation (i.e. ETOPS); and Any extra fuel required by the commander. In flight re-planning fuel calculation is to consider trip fuel for the remainder of the flight, reserve fuel consisting of: Contingency fuel; Alternate fuel; Final reserve fuel; Any additional fuel reserve required by the commander.

sections.title: Refuelling Procedures
sections.content: The refuelling of aeroplanes is not permitted with Avgas or wide cut fuels (e.g. Jet B - a mixture of gasoline and kerosene), when passengers are on board, embarking or disembarking. If other fuels are being used (e.g. Jet A1) then procedures are to be established to ensure that the aeroplane is properly manned by qualified personnel ready to initiate an immediate evacuation by the most expeditious means.

sections.title: Crew Members at Duty Stations
sections.content: Each member of the flight crew is to be at the designated duty station for take-off and landing. During all other phases of flight, each crew member is to remain alert and at the duty station, unless the duty in connection with the operation requires absence, or for physiological needs. At least one suitably qualified pilot is to be at the controls at all times. All cabin crew are to be seated at their assigned stations during critical phases of flight.

sections.title: Seats Safety Belts and Harnesses
sections.content: Each crew member is to be properly secured by all safety belts and harnesses for take-off and landing and at any other time the commander deems necessary. During other phases of flight, each flight crew member is to keep the safety belt fastened when seated at the duty station. Each passenger is to occupy a seat and the seat belt (or harness) is to be properly fastened before take-off and landing, during taxiing and whenever deemed necessary in the interest of safety by the commander.

sections.title: Smoking Regulations
sections.content: The commander is to prohibit smoking: Whenever deemed necessary in the interest of safety; While the aeroplane is on the ground (unless permitted in accordance with OM); Outside designated smoking areas, in the aisles and in the toilets; In cargo compartments or other areas where cargo is carried not stored in flame resistant containers; and In cabin areas where oxygen is being supplied.

sections.title: Ice and Other Contaminants
sections.content: The commander is not permitted to commence the take-off run unless all the surfaces of the aeroplane are clear of all deposits which might adversely affect the performance of the aeroplane. The operator is to establish procedures for the de-icing and anti-icing of aeroplanes.

sections.title: Use of Supplemental Oxygen
sections.content: The Commander is to ensure that all flight crew members performing essential duties use supplemental oxygen continuously whenever the cabin altitude exceeds 10 000 ft for a period in excess of 30 minutes, and at all times whenever the cabin altitude exceeds 13 000 ft.

sections.title: Ground Proximity Detection
sections.content: When undue proximity to the ground is detected by any flight crew member or warning system, the commander or pilot at the controls of the aeroplane is to take corrective action to establish a safe flight condition.

sections.title: Operating Limitations
sections.content: All weather operations are divided into categories based on the visibility and cloud ceiling. These include Visual Flight Rules (VFR), Special VFR, Instrument Flight Rules (IFR), and specific approach categories.

sections.title: Category I Approach
sections.content: A Category I approach is a precision approach using ILS, MLS, or PAR with a DH not below 200 ft and RVR not less than 550 m.

sections.title: Category II Approach
sections.content: A Category II approach is a precision approach with a DH not below 100 ft and RVR not less than 300 m.

sections.title: Category III Approach
sections.content: Category III approaches include IIIA (DH below 100 ft or no DH, RVR not less than 175 m), IIIB (RVR not less than 50 m), and IIIC (no DH, no RVR limitation).

sections.title: Low Visibility Procedures
sections.content: Low visibility procedures (LVP) are required when RVR is below 400 m. These procedures include specific taxiway lighting, runway protection, and air traffic control procedures.

sections.title: Runway Visual Range (RVR)
sections.content: RVR is the distance over which a pilot can see runway markings or lights from the cockpit. It is the primary factor in determining approach and landing minimums.

sections.title: Decision Height (DH)
sections.content: DH is the height at which a decision must be made to continue the approach or to execute a missed approach. Below DH, the approach must not continue unless the required visual reference is established.

sections.title: Equipment Requirements
sections.content: All weather operations require specific equipment including radio altimeters, ILS receivers, and in some cases, enhanced vision systems.

sections.title: Minimum Equipment List (MEL)
sections.content: The MEL defines the minimum equipment that must be operational for flight. It allows for operation with certain equipment inoperative provided appropriate procedures are followed.

sections.title: Configuration Deviation List (CDL)
sections.content: The CDL identifies non-essential equipment that may be missing without affecting airworthiness. It provides allowances for flight with certain items missing.

sections.title: Required Navigation Performance (RNP)
sections.content: RNP specifies the navigation accuracy required for specific operations. RNP operations require specific aircraft equipment and crew qualifications.

sections.title: Reduced Vertical Separation Minima (RVSM)
sections.content: RVSM allows reduced vertical separation of 1000 ft between FL290 and FL410. It requires specific equipment including height keeping systems.

sections.title: Traffic Collision Avoidance System (TCAS)
sections.content: TCAS is required for all aircraft operating in controlled airspace. It provides traffic advisories and resolution advisories to prevent mid-air collisions.

sections.title: Ground Proximity Warning System (GPWS)
sections.content: GPWS provides warnings of terrain contact and is required for all commercial transport aircraft. The system includes mode cautions, terrain proximity warnings, and glide slope warnings.

sections.title: Emergency Equipment
sections.content: Required emergency equipment includes life jackets, life rafts, fire extinguishers, first aid kits, emergency lighting, and megaphones.

sections.title: Required Navigation Performance (RNP)
sections.content: RNP specifies the navigation accuracy required for specific operations. RNP operations require specific aircraft equipment and crew qualifications. The RNP value defines the lateral accuracy requirement in nautical miles. RNAV operations require approved equipment and procedures.

sections.title: Reduced Vertical Separation Minima (RVSM)
sections.content: RVSM allows reduced vertical separation of 1000 ft between FL290 and FL410. It requires specific equipment including: Two independent barometric altimeters agreeing to within +/- 200 ft; Autopilot with height hold capability; Altitude deviation alerting system (minimum of an aural alert if the aircraft deviates by +/- 300 ft); SSR with altitude alerting mode (mode C). The altimeters must be checked prior to entering RVSM airspace.

sections.title: Traffic Collision Avoidance System (TCAS)
sections.content: TCAS is required for all aircraft operating in controlled airspace. It provides traffic advisories (TA) and resolution advisories (RA) to prevent mid-air collisions. The pilot must respond to RAs by following the vertical speed guidance. TCAS II is required for operations in the North Atlantic MNPSA.

sections.title: Area Navigation (RNAV)
sections.content: RNAV allows aircraft to operate on any desired flight path without reference to ground-based navigation aids. RNAV operations require approved navigation equipment capable of determining aircraft position using inputs from various navigation sensors (VOR/DME, INS, GPS).

sections.title: Crew Composition
sections.content: The minimum crew composition is specified in the operations manual and must include at least one pilot qualified as commander and any additional crew required for the type of operation.

sections.title: Flight Time Limitations
sections.content: Flight time limitations are specified to prevent fatigue. These include maximum flight time per duty period, per month, and per year, with mandatory rest periods between duties.

sections.title: Crew Records
sections.content: The operator shall maintain records for each crew member including training, qualifications, medical certificates, and flight time. Records must be retained for a specified period.

sections.title: Journey Log
sections.content: The journey log must record details of each flight including aircraft registration, crew names, times, routes, and any incidents or accidents.

sections.title: Technical Log
sections.content: The technical log records the airworthiness status of the aircraft including maintenance actions, defects, and maintenance releases.

sections.title: Mass and Balance Documentation
sections.content: Mass and balance documentation must be prepared for each flight showing the load distribution and confirmation that the aircraft is within limits.

sections.title: Duty Time Limitations
sections.content: Flight time limitations include maximum flight time per duty period (normally 13 hours for two-pilot operations), maximum flight time per month (normally 100 hours), and maximum flight time per year (normally 1000 hours). Mandatory rest periods between duties are required, with a minimum rest of 36 hours per week.

sections.title: Rest Periods
sections.content: Rest periods must be at least as long as the preceding duty period or 12 hours, whichever is greater. Minimum rest before a flight duty period is 12 hours, or 24 hours for extended reserve. Operators must schedule rest periods to prevent fatigue.

sections.title: Cabin Crew Requirements
sections.content: The minimum number of cabin crew is determined by the aircraft type and passenger capacity. For aircraft with more than 19 passenger seats, at least one cabin crew member per 50 passengers is required. Additional crew members may be required for specific operations.

chapter: Chapter 8 - Long Range Flight and Polar Navigation

sections.title: Long Range Operations
sections.content: Long range operations require special consideration of fuel, navigation, emergency procedures, and crew fatigue. Extended range operations may require ETOPS authorization.

sections.title: Polar Navigation
sections.content: Polar navigation presents unique challenges including magnetic compass unreliability, limited navigation aids, and extreme weather. Special procedures and equipment are required.

sections.title: ETOPS Requirements
sections.content: Extended Twin Engine Operations (ETOPS) require specific aircraft certification, operator approval, and special procedures. The aircraft must be able to reach an alternate aerodrome within specified time limits.

sections.title: Fuel Planning
sections.content: Long range fuel planning must include contingency fuel, alternate fuel, and additional reserves. Fuel consumption monitoring is essential throughout the flight.

sections.title: Crew Rest Facilities
sections.content: Long range operations require adequate crew rest facilities to manage fatigue. This includes bunks and rest areas for crew members on extended flights.

sections.title: Extended Range Operations
sections.content: Extended range operations require special consideration of fuel, navigation, emergency procedures, and crew fatigue. Extended range operations may require ETOPS authorization. The operator must maintain an ETOPS reliability programme and ensure appropriate alternate aerodromes are available.

sections.title: Polar Operations
sections.content: Polar navigation presents unique challenges including: Magnetic compass unreliability due to the weak horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field; Limited navigation aids; Extreme weather conditions; Ice crystal icing at altitude. Special procedures and equipment are required including gyro-referenced compasses and grid navigation.

sections.title: Navigation System Requirements
sections.content: For oceanic operations, two independent long-range navigation systems (LRNS) are required. Approved systems include GNSS (GPS), INS, and IRS. Each system must be capable of providing continuous position, track, and speed information. In the event of failure, procedures exist to determine which system is inaccurate or to navigate using assistance from other aircraft.

sections.title: Communications for Long Range
sections.content: Due to the distances involved, HF SSB voice is the primary method of communication in oceanic airspace. Long range VHF is available within 250 NM of land. SELCAL (Selective Calling) allows pilots to maintain watch without constant monitoring. 121.5 MHz is monitored for emergency purposes.

sections.title: Fuel Planning for Long Range
sections.content: Long range fuel planning must include: Trip fuel; Contingency fuel (normally 5% of trip fuel); Alternate fuel; Final reserve fuel (45 minutes for turbine engines); Additional fuel for ETOPS; Extra fuel required by the commander. Fuel consumption monitoring is essential throughout the flight.

chapter: Chapter 9 - Minimum Navigation Performance Specification Airspace (MNPSA)

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Due to economic, geographical and geophysical considerations, the airspace over the North Atlantic Ocean between the latitudes of 35N and 70N is some of the most frequently flown airspace in the World. Typically there could be up to 300 aircraft over the Ocean at the same time. More than 425,000 commercial aircraft alone cross the airspace annually. By agreement between the states bordering the North Atlantic (Canada; USA; Iceland; Greenland; Norway; the UK; Eire; France; Spain; Portugal) the ICAO North Atlantic Region (NAT region) has been established to provide a framework for the solution of problems concerned with transoceanic operations. Within the NAT region, the area over the ocean and northwards towards the North Pole is designated as airspace in which a minimum standard for air navigation has been specified. This is known as the NAT Minimum Navigation Performance Specification Airspace (MNPSA). ICAO publishes Doc 7030 containing the NAT Regional Supplements, and the North Atlantic MNPS Airspace OM covering specific NAT procedures.

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content: The problems are threefold: Firstly, the economic requirement is for traffic to fly to and from North America, mainly the United States and Canada, and Europe. The majority of this traffic is scheduled passenger operations and this falls into distinct flow patterns resulting in a tidal type of situation with peaks in each direction at certain times of the day. Secondly, the meteorological situation over the Ocean standardizes the polar frontal jet stream and the sub-tropical jet stream at about 55N and 40N respectively. The jet stream flow is always from west to east. Modern turbine powered aeroplanes are most efficient (fuel efficient) at the altitude where the air is coldest (densest) for engine performance, but least dense for drag reduction. The air is coldest at the tropopause. Hence, all the traffic flying across the North Atlantic will want to cruise at or about the tropopause. At 40N this will be about 40,000 ft whereas at 55N this will be about 35,000 ft. This tends to concentrate all the traffic at or about these altitudes. Thirdly, the total absence of ground based navigation aids means that navigation accuracy will not be as good as over land and allowances in separation will need to be made by the ATC authorities.

sections.title: References
sections.content: References include: ICAO North Atlantic Manual, ICAO Regional Air Navigation (RAN) Plan for the NAT Region, ICAO Regional Supplementary Procedures (Doc 7030).

sections.title: Transoceanic Navigation Problems
sections.content: The problems of navigating aeroplanes over vast areas of sea are no worse than the problems of doing the same over huge tracts of uninhabited land. These are: No aerodromes, No ground based radar, No fixed radio navigation beacons (VOR; NDB etc.), Outside of VHF radio range hence reliance on HF communications. Additionally, the North Atlantic suffers from abnormally high levels of traffic density with modern turbine engined aeroplanes wanting to fly at the same flight levels, and an economically generated tidal flow of traffic. As the NAT region encompasses latitudes higher than 70N problems are encountered with magnetic compasses due to the weak horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field (less than 6 microTesla) and occasionally large values of magnetic variation (31W at Resolute Bay in northern Canada). This last problem requires radio navigation beacons to be oriented to true north and at polar latitudes the use of grid navigation techniques using gyro referenced compasses.

sections.title: The Airspace
sections.content: The concentration of the traffic by route and altitude, together with the separation requirements and navigation system requirements, mean that all traffic flying across the North Atlantic is required to fly IFR. To this extent, all the airspace is classified as class A between FL55 and FL660. By definition, class A is controlled airspace (CAS) in which ATC is provided to IFR traffic therefore the airspace must, by definition, be a control area (CTA). This is the case but because special rules are applicable, the airspace is defined as an Oceanic Control Area (OCA). In practice, there are 5 NAT OCAs encompassing the MNPSA: Shanwick (controlled by Prestwick), Santa Maria (controlled by Lisbon), Gander (controlled by Gander), New York (controlled by New York), and Reykjavik (controlled by Reykjavik) with corresponding Oceanic Area Control Centres (OACCs) at Prestwick, Lisbon, Gander, New York and Reykjavik respectively. The OCAs are contiguous with the East/West boundary being at 30W (Shanwick and Gander) and 40W (Santa Maria and New York) and the North/South boundary being at 45N (Shanwick/Gander and Santa Maria/New York). Reykjavik extends from the northern boundaries of Shanwick and Gander to the North Pole, including the majority of Greenland. The easterly and westerly continental boundaries are defined by domestic airspace (national FIRs). The West Atlantic Route System (WATRS) is also in this area for traffic between USA and the Caribbean.

sections.title: MNPS Authority
sections.content: Operators of aircraft flying within the MNPSA are required to have authority approval (stated on the AOC) requiring the aircraft to be able to navigate in accordance with the relevant RNP. Approval for MNPS will be indicated to Air Traffic by inserting the letters SX in item 10 of the flight plan. Requested TRUE Mach number, taking into account known errors in the flight deck indication, is to be inserted in item 15 of the flight plan. Wherever possible this requested speed will be accommodated, however occasionally ATC may need to assign a Mach number 0.01 higher than, or 0.02 lower than requested. Pilots should maintain their last assigned Mach number during step-climbs in oceanic airspace. If due to aircraft performance this is not feasible ATC should be advised at the time of the request for the step climb. After leaving oceanic airspace pilots must maintain their assigned Mach number in domestic controlled airspace unless and until the appropriate ATC unit authorizes a change.

sections.title: RVSM
sections.content: MNPS Airspace extends from Flight Level (FL) 285-420, incorporating the RVSM FLs 290-410. Aircraft flying in MNPSA must also be authorized to fly RVSM, and must comply with the altimetry Minimum Aircraft System Performance Specification (MASPS). In domestic airspace RVSM FLs are allocated according to magnetic track (flights Easterly are on odd FL, 290, 310 etc, Westerly on even FL 300, 320 etc.) Due to the tidal nature of the majority of North Atlantic traffic during the times of the Organized Track System (OTS) all FL are available in both directions. Outside the OTS standard RVSM FL apply. The minimum equipment requirement for RVSM flight is as follows: Two independent barometric altimeters agreeing to within +/- 200 ft, Autopilot with height hold capability, Altitude deviation alerting system (minimum of an aural alert if the aircraft deviates by +/- 300 ft), SSR with altitude alerting mode (mode C). The altimeters must be checked prior to entering the NAT OCA. Pilots must report when reaching any new cruising level, and if a deviation of more than 300 ft occurs it must be reported to ATS, with a subsequent written report post flight.

sections.title: Navigation System Requirements
sections.content: The minimum equipment for flight in MNPS Airspace is 2 independent Long Range Navigation Systems (LRNS) to cater for failure of one system. Approved systems may be: GNSS (GPS), INS, IRS. Each system must be capable of providing continuous position, track and speed information. In addition, all turbine powered aeroplanes with MTOM > 5700 kg, or having more than 19 passenger seats are required to carry and operate ACAS II in the NAT region.

sections.title: Navigation System Serviceability
sections.content: The requirement for 2 LRNS covers the case of a failure in one system and each system must have a failure warning indication. Where triple INs are used as an IRS, a voting system is employed to ensure use of the most accurate information. In a double INS system, external information (heading and drift) will be required to determine which system is inaccurate if a failure occurs. In the event of total failure, ask another aircraft for assistance or follow the contrails. In the event of total navigation system failure, declare an emergency using MAYDAY or PAN PAN procedures.

sections.title: NAT Tracks
sections.content: The most desirable routes are the minimum time tracks (MTT) from Paris, London and central Europe terminating at New York, Chicago, Atlanta, Montreal and Boston. Generally, an MTT is a great circle track with the most favourable wind. To accommodate all these flights going the same way at approximately the same time, a set of roughly parallel tracks is established with lateral separation based on the MNPS RNP. The FLs allocated to these tracks are RVSM levels and to facilitate yet further utilization of the vertical airspace, the tracks are made effectively one way only and both the eastbound and the westbound semi-circular RVSM levels are allocated to the track direction. These tracks are called organized tracks and the overall concept is called the organized track system (OTS). Whilst this appears to be much formalized in concept, the use of the tracks is not mandatory, and an operator may plan any route required. A route that does not comply with the existing OTS is called a random route.

sections.title: OTS Track Designation
sections.content: To accommodate the tidal nature of the transatlantic flow, two OTS are established: the daytime OTS westbound, and the night time OTS eastbound. Each track in the OTS is given an individual identifier or designator. For the daytime OTS the tracks are lettered from A for the most northerly at the start point and then sequentially lettered in a southerly direction. For the night time OTS, the most southerly track is Z with the next track to the north Y etc.

sections.title: OTS Changeover
sections.content: At some time of the day the eastbound OTS will be replaced with the westbound OTS and vice versa. The OTS period is defined at 30W (approximately the midpoint for most traffic). The standard OTS periods of validity are: Daytime (Westbound) OTS 1130-1900UTC at 30W, Night-time (Eastbound) OTS 0100-0800UTC at 30W. These times occasionally change however, any changes will be highlighted in the remarks of the Track Message. The changeover periods are 0801Z to 1129Z and 1901Z to 0059Z.

sections.title: OTS Message (Track Message - TM)
sections.content: At set times each day, the airspace managers of the 5 OCAs together with Met advisers hold a conference to decide the following day's OTS. As well as considering the weather they will take into account route requests from operators, who can request specified routings by submitting a Preferred Route Message (PRM) in advance. The manager of Shanwick is then responsible for publishing the next daytime (westbound) OTS and the Gander manager publishes the night time (eastbound) OTS. The OTS is then distributed to interested parties in the form of an OTS message which specifies the date; period of validity; the routes (Lat/Long positions and named positions) by designator, and remarks including the track message identifier (TMI) and notices regarding airspace reservations and navigational data.

sections.title: Track Message Identifier (TMI)
sections.content: The TMI is a method of identifying the OTS promulgated by the OACC. It consists of the name of the OACC, the abbreviation TMI followed by the Julian date of the OTS. The Julian calendar starts at 001 for 1 January and ends at 365 for 31 December (366 for a leap year). So, the eastbound OTS for 28 February is Gander TMI 059. If any amendment is required, the whole NAT track message will be re-issued. An added note will indicate the revision with an alphabetic character, e.g. TMI059A, then B, etc.

sections.title: Track Routings
sections.content: After the track identifier, the first position in a NAT track is the entry point. This can be either a Lat/Long (a 5-character group giving the whole number of degrees of latitude, followed by a slash, then the whole number of degrees longitude e.g. 59/10 meaning 5900N01000W) or a named position (the ICAO standard for position names is used - 5 letters e.g. CIMAT). The rest of the route, in the 5-character lat/long form and named positions if applicable, follows. An example of a westbound route is as follows: A 59/10 61/20 61/30 61/40 61/50 60/60 CIMAT. The route is route A therefore it is the most northerly of that OTS. The entry point is 59N 10W. From there the route is 61N 20W; 61N 30W; 61N 40W; 61N 50W; 60N 60W and exit at point CIMAT.

sections.title: Allocation of FLs
sections.content: In theory, all the RVSM FLs (290 - 410 inclusive) should be available for allocation. In practice this is not always the case. For the more popular routes more FLs are usually available than for the less popular. Generally, FLs 290 and 300 are too low for normal traffic and FLs 400 and 410 just a touch too high. Also the freeing of RVSM FLs to non-OTS traffic is in keeping with the objective of making as much airspace available for non-NAT Track traffic as possible, and also offers flexibility for random routes. For example: EAST LVLS NIL; WEST LVLS 310 320 330 340 350 360 390. The inclusion of EAST LVLS NIL implies that for the period of the OTS all other direction traffic along this route would be random route traffic.

sections.title: Domestic Routes
sections.content: The TM also includes details of specific routing from domestic airspace to the entry point for the route and also from the exit point into domestic airspace. This will allow domestic airspace managers to flow manage transatlantic traffic into the domestic airways or high-level route system within the receiving FIR. For example: EUR RTS WEST NIL; NAR N464B N466B N468B N472B N474B. This implies that there are no specified European routes to the start point, and from CIMAT the NAR (North American routings) are N464B etc.

sections.title: Random Routes
sections.content: As the use of the OTS is not mandatory, operators may plan to use any other route. Such routes are called random routes. Examples of a random route could be: the reciprocal of an OTS track; a track that follows part of an OTS track; a route that crosses 30W outside of the relevant OTS; a track that is completely outside of the OTS or a track that crosses one or more OTS tracks. In any event, operators are required to consider the implications of planning such routes and the OACCs will apply whatever restrictions are necessary to random routes to protect the OTS traffic.

sections.title: Polar Track Structure (PTS)
sections.content: The PTS no longer exists.

sections.title: Route Structures Adjacent to NAT MNPS Airspace
sections.content: Other routes within NAT MNPS Airspace are: Blue Spruce Routes (established as special routes for aircraft equipped with only one serviceable LRNS), routes between Northern Europe and Spain/Canaries/Lisbon FIR, routings between the Azores and the Portuguese mainland and between the Azores and the Madeira Archipelago, routings between Iceland and Constable Pynt on the east coast of Greenland and between Kook Islands on the west coast of Greenland and Canada, special routes of short stage lengths where aircraft equipped with normal short-range navigation equipment can meet the MNPS track-keeping criteria.

sections.title: Communications
sections.content: Due to the distances involved, the present primary method of communication is HF SSB voice. Long range VHF is available to aircraft when within 250 NM of land, and VHF is also used for the delivery of oceanic clearances to aircraft prior to entering the OCAs. There is also a VHF air-to-air frequency (123.45 MHz) to allow an aircraft experiencing any communications or navigation problems to talk to another aircraft. All aircraft flying on NAT routes are also required to monitor 121.5 MHz. Routine air/ground ATS Voice communications in the NAT Region are conducted via aeradio stations staffed by communicators who have no executive ATC authority. Messages are relayed by the ground station to/from the air traffic controllers in the relevant OAC.

sections.title: HF
sections.content: Long range HF communications is not user friendly. The correct choice of frequency is crucial and this is time-of-day dependent. In common with all aeronautical HF networks around the world, the NAT region utilizes families of frequencies. This means that all or selected ground stations monitor the same frequency. The frequencies used are 3, 5, 8, 11 and 13 MHz. The general rule is that to talk to a station 1000 NM away you will need a higher frequency during the day than at night (the higher the Sun, the higher the frequency). The first HF frequency to use with the air-ground station will be specified in the oceanic clearance. The ground radio operator will be monitoring all the frequencies in the family so with the first call to the air-ground station the frequency being used is indicated by using the following phraseology: Shanwick Speedbird 289 on 88. This would tell the ground operator that you are calling him on 8864 MHz.

sections.title: SELCAL
