sections.content: When using HF communications and even when using ADS-C and/or CPDLC, pilots should maintain a listening watch on the assigned frequency, unless SELCAL is fitted, in which case they should ensure the following sequence of actions: Provide the SELCAL code in the flight plan; Check the operation of the SELCAL equipment, at or prior to entry into oceanic airspace, with the appropriate aeradio station; Maintain thereafter a SELCAL watch. It is important to note that it is equally essential to comply with the SELCAL provisions even if SATCOM Voice or ADS/CPDLC are being used for routine air/ground ATS communications. This will ensure that ATC has a timely means of contacting the aircraft.

sections.title: Position Reports
sections.content: The system of ATC used across the NAT region is procedural ATC, which relies on pilot position reports to confirm the separation. At specified points pilots are required to make position reports. For an east/west route this is going to be at approximately every 500 NM. This meets the ICAO requirement for position reports to be made at intervals not greater than 1 hour. Standard air/ground message types and formats are used within the NAT Region and are published in State AIPs and Atlantic Orientation charts. All times in any of the messages should be expressed in hours and minutes UTC. Above 70N the position reports are made at intervals of 20 degrees of longitude.

sections.title: Initial Clearance
sections.content: At least 40 minutes before entering the NAT airspace (above FL55) pilots are required to obtain an oceanic clearance directly from the initial OACC. The clearance request should include requested FL, and maximum acceptable FL at the entry to the OCA. As all the entry points to the OTS are within 250 NM of the coast, the aircraft should be able to obtain the clearance by VHF. If direct communications are not possible, HF can be used through the air-ground station. Alternatively, a request could be made through the domestic ACC on the airways/UIR frequency in use. The clearance will include the limit of clearance (normally the destination), the track identifier, the entry point, the initial FL, the Mach number required and any specific instructions. This must be read back, but an abbreviated read back is permitted replacing the route details with the track identifier plus the TMI.

sections.title: Transition
sections.content: Because the OTS uses all the available FLs as one way streets when an aircraft leaves the OTS and joins the domestic route system an adjustment in FL may be required. In order to make sure this is only conducted where there is no loss of separation, special areas known as transition areas have been established for this purpose.

sections.title: SSR
sections.content: All aircraft operating in NAT MNPS Airspace must have a serviceable SSR transponder with Mode C capability. This enables ATC to identify aircraft and receive level information.

sections.title: Meteorological Reports
sections.content: Meteorological reports are essential for flight planning in the NAT region. Pilots should obtain updated weather information before entering oceanic airspace and monitor en-route weather reports.

sections.title: Special Contingencies and Procedures
sections.content: Special contingencies may require deviation from the cleared route or level. In such cases, pilots must advise ATC as soon as possible and obtain revised clearance if able.

sections.title: Strategic Lateral Offset Procedure (SLOP)
sections.content: SLOP provides a means of reducing the risk of collision in oceanic airspace. Aircraft may offset track by 1 or 2 NM to the right to provide lateral separation from other aircraft at the same level.

sections.title: Deviation Around Severe Weather
sections.content: When severe weather is encountered, pilots may request clearance to deviate. If unable to obtain revised clearance, the pilot may deviate up to 15 NM right of track and climb or descend 500 ft as per procedures.

sections.title: Unable to Obtain Revised Clearance
sections.content: If unable to continue in accordance with clearance, the pilot should: Attempt to obtain a re-clearance. If unable to obtain re-clearance, turn to take up a track offset by 15 NM and climb 500 ft or descend 500 ft as appropriate. If at FL410, climb 300 m (1000 ft) or descend 150 m (500 ft). An aeroplane unable to maintain its assigned flight level should initially minimize its descent rate to the extent that it is operationally feasible, turn while descending to acquire and maintain in either direction a track laterally separated by 15 NM from its assigned route or track, and for the subsequent level flight, a level should be selected which differs from those normally used by 300 m (1000 ft) if above FL410 or by 150 m (500 ft) if below FL410.

sections.title: Navigation System Failure
sections.content: If a failure occurs before entering the MNPSA the pilot should attempt to resolve the problem (determine that the system is useable) or land (or delay the take-off) and get the system repaired. Under no circumstances should an aircraft enter the MNPSA with unresolved navigation errors. Alternatively, the pilot may consider obtaining a clearance to remain outside MNPSA, e.g. below FL290, or re-file for one of the special routes designed for non-compliant aircraft. If however, the aircraft has triple LRNS, the MNPSA requirement for two LRNS would be met with one unserviceable, and the flight could proceed as planned. If an aircraft with only two LRNSs suffers a system failure after entering the MNPSA, the flight is to be continued in accordance with the clearance received. In this case the pilot should assess the situation, prepare a proposal to put to the OACC, advise and consult with the OACC, and obtain appropriate re-clearance prior to deviation from the previous clearance. If the remaining system fails after entering the MNPSA, the pilot should notify the OACC immediately, make best use of the information obtained from another aircraft, keep a good look out for other aircraft, make maximum use of exterior lights, consider climbing or descending 500 ft, and revert to manual navigation if the navigation computer has failed.

sections.title: Errors Associated with Oceanic Clearances
sections.content: Navigation errors associated with oceanic clearances fall into several categories: ATC system loop errors and waypoint insertion errors. An ATC system loop error is any error caused by a misunderstanding between the pilot and the controller regarding the assigned flight level, Mach number or route to be followed. Such errors can arise from incorrect interpretation of the NAT track message by dispatchers, errors in co-ordination between OACs, or misinterpretation by pilots of oceanic clearances. Experience has shown that many of the track-keeping errors in the NAT MNPS Airspace occur as a result of crews programming the navigation system(s) with incorrect waypoint data. These are referred to as waypoint insertion errors and frequently originate from: Failure to observe the principles of checking waypoints to be inserted in the navigation systems, against the ATC cleared route, Failure to load waypoint information carefully, Failure to cross-check on-board navigation systems.

sections.title: Pre-flight and In-flight Procedures
sections.content: The pre-flight procedures for any NAT MNPS flight must include a UTC time check and re-synchronization of the master clock. Operators must ensure that flight crew are familiar with the concept of cross-checking route inputs into the FMS. Ideally the route would be loaded independently (each pilot loading his own FMS if this is the type of system installed) then cross-checked by the other pilot. This should be done by noting the position in the system, then comparing it to the Master Document, rather than the other way around. Alignment of the IRS, or Satellite Availability Prediction Programme, for flights using GNSS, must be ensured, and an altimeter cross-check conducted. Prior to entering the NAT OCA a check of the SELCAL, master clock and altimeter cross-check must be conducted. The altimeters must be within 200 ft of each other, and the standby altimeter difference should be noted, so that the error can be applied should there be a failure of the primary systems. The altimeters are to be compared at each subsequent waypoint, and disagreements in excess of 200 ft must be reported.

chapter: Chapter 10 - Special Operational Procedures and Hazards

sections.title: Volcanic Activity
sections.content: Volcanic ash poses significant hazard to aircraft. Pilots must avoid volcanic ash clouds and report encounters to ATC. Special procedures apply for operations in areas of volcanic activity.

sections.title: Mountain Wave Operations
sections.content: Mountain wave activity can create severe turbulence and updrafts/downdrafts. Operations in mountainous areas require special consideration of wind conditions and turbulence.

sections.title: Operations in Icing Conditions
sections.content: Airframe icing requires specific procedures and equipment. Anti-icing and de-icing procedures must be followed when operating in icing conditions.

sections.title: Low Level Operations
sections.content: Low level operations require special consideration of terrain, obstacles, and weather. Minimum safe altitudes must be maintained and obstacle clearance ensured.

sections.title: Helicopter Operations
sections.content: Helicopter operations near helipads and offshore platforms require special procedures for approach, landing, and go-around.

sections.title: Volcanic Ash Procedures
sections.content: Volcanic ash poses significant hazard to aircraft including: Engine malfunction from abrasive particles; Visibility reduction; Aircraft performance degradation; Communication and navigation equipment interference. Pilots must avoid volcanic ash clouds and report encounters to ATC. Operations in areas of volcanic activity require pre-flight briefing on ash dispersal forecasts.

sections.title: Mountain Wave Operations
sections.content: Mountain wave activity can create severe turbulence and strong updrafts/downdrafts. Operations in mountainous areas require special consideration of: Wind speed and direction at mountain top; Stability of the atmosphere; Wave length and amplitude. Severe turbulence may be encountered on the lee side of mountains. Pilots should avoid operations in areas of mountain wave activity.

sections.title: Operations in Icing Conditions
sections.content: Airframe icing requires specific procedures and equipment. Anti-icing and de-icing procedures must be followed when operating in icing conditions. Clear ice is particularly dangerous as it disrupts airflow. Pilots must activate anti-ice systems before entering visible moisture and maintain them until exiting icing conditions.

sections.title: Low Level Operations
sections.content: Low level operations require special consideration of: Terrain and obstacle clearance; Minimum safe altitudes; Weather conditions; Emergency procedures. All low level flights must maintain terrain clearance in accordance with applicable regulations. Operations below 500 ft AGL require specific authorization.

sections.title: Fire Detection
sections.content: Fire detection systems are required in cargo compartments, engines, and lavatories. Smoke detection is required in baggage compartments and avionics bays.

sections.title: Fire Fighting Equipment
sections.content: Fire fighting equipment includes portable extinguishers, fixed extinguishers in cargo compartments and engines, and protective breathing equipment for crew.

sections.title: Procedures for Fire
sections.content: In case of fire, the crew must immediately take action to extinguish the fire. This includes using fire extinguishers, shutting down systems, and initiating emergency procedures.

sections.title: Smoke Control
sections.content: Smoke control procedures include using ventilation systems, closing air recycle valves, and donning oxygen masks. Crew must be prepared for smoke evacuation.

sections.title: Emergency Exit Procedures
sections.content: In case of fire requiring evacuation, the crew must be prepared to operate emergency exits. Training includes door operation, slide deployment, and passenger evacuation.

sections.title: Fire Detection Systems
sections.content: Fire detection systems are required in cargo compartments, engines, and lavatories. Smoke detection is required in baggage compartments and avionics bays. Warning is given to the flight crew via visual and aural indicators on the flight deck.

sections.title: Fire Fighting Equipment
sections.content: Fire fighting equipment includes: Portable extinguishers (halon or equivalent) located in the flight deck and passenger compartments; Fixed extinguishers in cargo compartments and engines; Protective breathing equipment (PBE) for crew. All crew members must be trained in the use of fire fighting equipment.

sections.title: Procedures for Fire
sections.content: In case of fire, the crew must immediately: Identify the source of the fire using detection systems; Attempt to extinguish the fire using appropriate extinguishers; Shut down affected systems as required; Initiate emergency procedures as required; Inform ATC of the emergency. The decision to land as soon as possible must be made for any fire that cannot be definitively extinguished.

sections.title: Smoke Control
sections.content: Smoke control procedures include: Using ventilation systems to control smoke flow; Closing air recycle valves to prevent smoke recirculation; Donning oxygen masks if smoke is present. Crew must be prepared for smoke evacuation. The no smoking sign is to be illuminated.

sections.title: Lavatory Fire
sections.content: Lavatory fires are often caused by faulty waste receptacles. The crew must: Use the built-in fire suppression system if available; Use portable extinguishers if necessary; Consider the need for an emergency landing. Warning is given by the smoke detector.

sections.title: Cargo Compartment Fire
sections.content: Cargo compartment fires require: Activation of the fire suppression system; Decision to land as soon as possible; Notification to ATC. Cargo compartment fire suppression systems are designed to contain the fire until landing can be achieved.

sections.title: Pressurization System
sections.content: Pressurization systems maintain a safe cabin altitude. A loss of pressurization can result in rapid decompression and hypoxia.

sections.title: Rapid Decompression
sections.content: Rapid decompression is a dangerous emergency requiring immediate action. The crew must don oxygen masks and descend to a safe altitude.

sections.title: Emergency Descent
sections.content: An emergency descent is required when cabin altitude cannot be controlled. The aircraft must descend to below 10,000 ft as quickly as possible.

sections.title: Hypoxia
sections.content: Hypoxia is a lack of oxygen in the body. Symptoms include confusion, euphoria, and loss of consciousness. Oxygen must be used immediately when pressurization is lost.

sections.title: Oxygen Requirements
sections.content: Oxygen supplies are required for crew and passengers for emergency descent. Portable oxygen is required for first aid and emergency procedures.

sections.title: Cabin Pressurization System
sections.content: Pressurization systems maintain a safe cabin altitude. Cabin altitude is typically maintained at 8000 ft or lower during cruise. A loss of pressurization can result in rapid decompression (explosive) or slow decompression. Both require immediate action.

sections.title: Rapid Decompression
sections.content: Rapid decompression is a dangerous emergency occurring when cabin pressure rapidly equalizes with ambient pressure. The rate of decompression can cause temporary hearing difficulty, wind debris, and fogging. The crew must don oxygen masks immediately and descend to a safe altitude below 10,000 ft.

sections.title: Emergency Descent
sections.content: An emergency descent is required when cabin altitude cannot be controlled. The aircraft must descend to below 10,000 ft as quickly as possible. The crew must don oxygen masks immediately, establish the aircraft in a safe descent, and inform ATC of the emergency. Rapid descent to 10,000 ft provides adequate oxygen for all occupants.

sections.title: Hypoxia
sections.content: Hypoxia is a lack of oxygen in the body. Symptoms include: Confusion and disorientation; Euphoria and overconfidence; Visual disturbances; Loss of consciousness. Hypoxia is insidious as the victim may not recognize symptoms. Oxygen must be used immediately when pressurization is lost.

sections.title: Oxygen Supply Requirements
sections.content: Oxygen supplies are required for: All passengers for the duration of descent from flight altitude; Flight crew members performing essential duties continuously above 10,000 ft cabin altitude for more than 30 minutes, and at all times above 13,000 ft cabin altitude; Emergency conditions requiring descent through altitudes above 10,000 ft.

sections.title: Physiological Symptoms
sections.content: At cabin altitudes above 10,000 ft: The onset of hypoxia begins; Night vision deteriorates above 5000 ft. Above 15,000 ft: Extreme drowsiness occurs. Above 20,000 ft: Unconsciousness occurs within minutes without oxygen. Above 25,000 ft: Loss of consciousness within 3-5 minutes.

sections.title: Windshear Definition
sections.content: Windshear is a sudden change in wind speed or direction. It can be horizontal or vertical and can occur at any altitude but is most dangerous near the ground.

sections.title: Microburst
sections.content: A microburst is a small but powerful downdraft causing severe windshear. It is particularly dangerous during take-off and landing as it causes sudden loss of performance.

sections.title: Detection
sections.content: Windshear can be detected using weather radar, windshear detection systems, and pilot reports. ATC will provide windshear alerts when reported by pilots.

sections.title: Avoidance
sections.content: The best method of avoiding windshear is to not fly through it. Pilots should avoid thunderstorms and areas of reported windshear.

sections.title: Recovery Procedures
sections.content: If windshear is encountered, the pilot must: Maintain take-off thrust or go-around power; Pitch for adequate climb attitude; Do not chase airspeed - maintain pitch attitude; Accept speed variations; Report the windshear to ATC. The key is to maintain a safe pitch attitude and not attempt to maintain a specific airspeed during windshear encounter.

sections.title: Microburst Characteristics
sections.content: A microburst is a small but powerful downdraft causing severe windshear. It is typically less than 1 NM in diameter and lasts less than 15 minutes. It causes a rapid change from headwind to tailwind, resulting in significant loss of performance. Microbursts are often associated with convective activity.

sections.title: Microburst Effects
sections.content: During a microburst encounter, the pilot experiences: First a strong headwind ( increasing performance); Then a strong downdraft and tailwind (decreasing performance dramatically); A rapid and significant airspeed loss near the surface. The pilot must recognize the situation and not attempt to climb until clear of the downdraft.

sections.title: Windshear Detection Methods
sections.content: Windshear can be detected using: Weather radar (detects convective activity); Windshear detection systems installed on aircraft (predictive windshear); Windshear detection systems at aerodromes (LLWAS); Pilot reports (PIREPs). ATC will provide windshear alerts when reported by pilots.

sections.title: Windshear Avoidance
sections.content: The best method of avoiding windshear is to not fly through it. Pilots should: Avoid thunderstorms; Avoid areas of reported windshear; Request deviations from ATC; Use weather radar. When take-off or landing during conditions favoring windshear, be prepared for immediate action.

sections.title: Take-off Windshear
sections.content: During take-off in windshear conditions: Apply maximum take-off thrust; Maintain a safe pitch attitude and do not chase airspeed; If unable to climb safely, consider aborting the take-off. If windshear is encountered after take-off, continue and do not attempt to turnaround unless clear of the weather.

sections.title: Landing Windshear
sections.content: During landing in windshear conditions: Approach at higher than normal speed; Do not attempt to descend on the glideslope if airspeed is low; If below glideslope, add power and go around; Be prepared to maintain the approach speed plus corrections.

sections.title: Wake Turbulence Generation
sections.content: Wake turbulence is generated by all aircraft as a product of lift. It consists of counter-rotating vortices that trail behind the wings.

sections.title: Vortex Strength
sections.content: Vortex strength is proportional to aircraft weight, speed, and wing span. Heavy, slow, and large aircraft generate the strongest vortices.

sections.title: Wake Categories
sections.content: Aircraft are categorized by weight: Light (L), Medium (M), Heavy (H), and Super (J). Separation requirements increase with category difference.

sections.title: Separation Requirements
sections.content: ATC provides separation between aircraft based on wake turbulence categories. Pilots should request increased separation when concerned about wake turbulence.

sections.title: Avoidance Procedures
sections.content: Pilots can avoid wake turbulence by staying above the flight path of the preceding aircraft, avoiding following closely, and being aware of vortex settling.

sections.title: Crosswind Effects
sections.content: Crosswinds can cause wake vortices to drift and mix. Pilots should be particularly aware of crosswinds from the left when landing behind larger aircraft. Light wind conditions cause vortices to remain on the runway longer. Strong crosswinds can cause rapid drift and dispersion.

sections.title: Vortex Generation
sections.content: Wake vortices are generated from the wing tips whenever lift is being produced. The strength of the vortex is proportional to aircraft weight, speed, and wing configuration. Heavier, slower aircraft with full flaps generate strongest vortices. Vortices settle below the flight path and can descend up to 900 ft/min.

sections.title: Vortex Circulation Pattern
sections.content: The circulation pattern shows that when viewed from behind, the left vortex rotates clockwise (upward on the outside) and the right vortex rotates counter-clockwise. This creates a situation where the vortices move outward from the centerline at low levels.

sections.title: Weight Load Speed Relationship
sections.content: Heavier aircraft create stronger vortices. For a given weight, slower speeds create stronger vortices. Take-off and landing configuration affects vortex strength. Boeing 747 and A380 are classified as Super aircraft.

sections.title: Separation Requirements
sections.content: ATC provides separation based on wake turbulence categories: Light behind Light - 2 NM; Medium behind Light - 3 NM; Heavy behind Light - 4 NM; Heavy behind Medium - 5 NM; Super behind Heavy - 6 NM. Pilots should request increased separation when concerned.

sections.title: Vortex Avoidance Procedures
sections.content: Pilots can avoid wake turbulence by: Staying above the flight path of the preceding aircraft; Not following in close formation; Requesting revised clearance; Landing beyond the touchdown zone of the preceding aircraft. Be particularly aware of wake vortex location during take-off and landing.

sections.title: Crosswind Effects on Vortices
sections.content: Crosswinds can cause wake vortices to drift and mix. A left crosswind on approach can push the wake turbulence onto the runway. Pilots should be particularly aware of crosswinds from the left when landing behind larger aircraft.

sections.title: Helicopter Wake Turbulence
sections.content: Helicopters create downwash vortices that are normally less significant than fixed wing aircraft. However, heavy helicopters creating significant downwash should be treated with similar separation as fixed wing aircraft.

sections.title: Security Requirements
sections.content: Security measures are required to prevent unlawful interference with aircraft. This includes screening of passengers, crew, and baggage.

sections.title: Unauthorized Access
sections.content: Access to aircraft must be controlled at all times. Only authorized persons with appropriate identification may have access to the aircraft.

sections.title: Bomb Threats
sections.content: In case of bomb threat, the crew must follow established procedures including search, reporting, and decision making for continuation or diversion.

sections.title: Unlawful Interference
sections.content: In case of unlawful interference, the crew must attempt to communicate with the hijacker, follow their instructions while attempting to resolve the situation.

sections.title: Cabin Security
sections.content: The flight crew should secure the cockpit door and prepare cabin crew for possible security situations. Communication procedures should be established.

sections.title: Security Programme
sections.content: An operator shall ensure that a security programme is established and maintained. This includes security training for all personnel and procedures for the prevention of unlawful interference. The programme aim is to minimize the opportunities for such interference.

sections.title: Reporting Unlawful Interference
sections.content: Following an act of unlawful interference, the commander or operator shall submit a report as soon as practicable to the local Authority and to the Authority in the state of the operator. The report shall include: Description of the incident; Actions taken; Outcome.

sections.title: Bomb Search Procedures
sections.content: An operator shall ensure that there is on board a checklist of the procedures to be followed in search of a bomb or improvised explosive device (IED) in case of suspected sabotage and for inspecting aeroplanes for concealed weapons, explosives or other dangerous devices. The checklist shall be supported by guidance on the appropriate course of action.

sections.title: Least-Risk Bomb Location
sections.content: The least-risk bomb location is the preferred place on the aircraft to place any suspect or confirmed IEDs. This location is specific to each aircraft type and is designed to minimize the risk to the aircraft and occupants in the event of detonation.

sections.title: Flight Deck Security
sections.content: Flight deck doors on commercial air transport aircraft must be capable of being locked and unlocked from the inside. There must be a means available for the cabin crew to advise the flight crew of suspicious activity. The door must be closed prior to engine start and remain so until after engine shutdown.

sections.title: Security Measures
sections.content: The door and surrounding bulkhead must be bullet proof and resistant to penetration by shrapnel. On aeroplanes with more than 60 passenger seats or with a MTOM over 45,500 kg, the door must be capable of being remotely locked/unlocked from either pilot station. A means to monitor the area immediately outside the door must be available to both pilots.

sections.title: Emergency Landing
sections.content: An emergency landing is one carried out when the aircraft cannot continue normal flight. It may be ditching or landing on land.

sections.title: Precautionary Landing
sections.content: A precautionary landing is one carried out as a planned action before the emergency becomes critical. It may be made at an aerodrome or suitable area.

sections.title: Ditching
sections.content: Ditching may be necessary when landing on land is not possible. It requires specific procedures for approach, ditching, and evacuation.

sections.title: Landing Area Selection
sections.content: For emergency or precautionary landing, the crew should select the best available area considering terrain, wind, obstacles, and accessibility for rescue.

sections.title: Briefing and Preparation
sections.content: Before an emergency landing, the crew must brief passengers, secure the cabin, and prepare for evacuation. Emergency equipment should be readied.

sections.title: Emergency Landing Definitions
sections.content: An emergency landing is one carried out when the aircraft cannot continue normal flight. It may be a ditching (landing on water) or landing on land. The variety of situations range from minor restrictions of performance to major or catastrophic structural failures or total propulsion system failures.

sections.title: Ditching Procedures
sections.content: Ditching is a deliberate landing on the surface of the sea. During design, the ditching characteristics of the aeroplane are explored fully and final attitudes, speeds and configurations suggested that would give the best chance of the airframe surviving. Statistics show 88% of ditchings result in few, if any, injuries. Success depends on rapid rescue and good communications.

sections.title: Ditching Preparation
sections.content: For a successful ditching: Passengers must wear life jackets but NOT inflate them until outside the aircraft; Adopt a braced posture whilst securely restrained; Loose articles must be stowed; Seats correctly positioned; Access to emergency exits cleared. The flight deck crew will action ditching checklists.

sections.title: Ditching Technique
sections.content: It is recommended to land along the swell direction, on the crest of the swell, at the lowest possible speed (gear up) with an attitude such that the tail will touch first. The aeroplane should be flown onto the water, not dropped onto it through stalling. There will be one or two minor skips before the main impact.

sections.title: Precautionary Landing
sections.content: A precautionary landing is one carried out as a planned action before the emergency becomes critical. It may be made at an aerodrome or suitable area. ATC should be informed of the decision to divert, the nature of the emergency, and the assistance required. Emergency communication procedures (Mayday/Pan Pan) should be employed.

sections.title: Passenger Briefing During Emergency
sections.content: In any emergency situation, fear amongst passengers is inevitable. The best way to overcome this is to brief the passengers fully about what has happened, what is being done, and what is likely to happen. A continual stream of advice and instructions will occupy the minds of passengers and prepare them mentally and physically.

sections.title: Evacuation Procedures
sections.content: Rapid evacuation is essential. The aircraft must be stopped and all engines shut down before ordering an evacuation. Successful evacuation relies on firm control by the cabin crew, imposition of strict discipline, and correct use of equipment. Aeroplanes with more than 44 passenger seats must be capable of being evacuated in less than 90 seconds.

sections.title: Megaphone Requirements
sections.content: An operator shall not operate an aeroplane with a maximum approved passenger seating configuration of more than 60 and carrying one or more passengers unless it is equipped with portable battery-powered megaphones. For 61-99 passengers: 1 megaphone required. For 100 or more passengers: 2 megaphones required.

sections.title: Fuel Jettison Requirements
sections.content: Fuel jettison may be required when the aircraft must land before sufficient fuel has been burned to reach the landing mass limit.

sections.title: Jettison Procedures
sections.content: Fuel jettison must be conducted at a safe altitude and over suitable areas. The aircraft must be in a controlled configuration.

sections.title: Fuel Jettison Systems
sections.content: Fuel jettison systems may be manual or automatic. They must be designed to prevent ignition of fuel vapors and ensure safe dispersion.

sections.title: Safety Considerations
sections.content: Fuel jettison must be coordinated with ATC. It should be conducted over unpopulated areas when possible to minimize environmental impact.

sections.title: Jettison System Requirements
sections.content: Every aeroplane must have a fuel jettison system fitted unless the maximum landing mass exceeds the maximum take-off mass less the mass of fuel necessary to carry out a 15 minute flight consisting of a take-off, climb to safe height, go-around and landing at the aerodrome of departure.

sections.title: Jettison Certification
sections.content: Where a fuel jettison system is required, the system must be capable of jettisoning enough fuel in 15 minutes (starting at max take-off mass) to reduce the aeroplane mass to enable the aeroplane to meet the climb requirements. Prior to certification, jettison trials must demonstrate that the system is free from fire hazards, discharges are clear of the aeroplane, and the operation does not affect controllability.

sections.title: Jettison Procedures
sections.content: The decision to jettison rests with the commander alone. When an aircraft operating within controlled airspace needs to dump fuel, the flight crew shall coordinate with ATC: the route to be flown (preferably over water and away from cities/towns); the level to be used (not less than 6000 ft); and the duration of the fuel dumping.

sections.title: Jettison Safety Procedures
sections.content: Unless there is an overriding requirement to jettison fuel, the aeroplane should be flown to an allocated area at an allocated height prior to commencing jettison. The weather conditions should be considered and areas of electrical storm activity should be avoided. The no smoking light is to be illuminated and passengers briefed.

sections.title: Jettison Operations
sections.content: During jettison: ATC is to be informed that jettison is about to commence; Electrical switching should be restricted to essential use only; HF radio transmission is to be suspended; VHF transmissions restricted to emergency/flight safety communications only; The flow of fuel from the jettison vents is to be visually monitored; Manoeuvres should be smooth.

chapter: Chapter 18 - Transport of Dangerous Goods by Air

sections.title: Dangerous Goods Classification
sections.content: Dangerous goods are classified into classes including explosives, gases, flammable liquids, flammable solids, oxidizers, and radioactive materials.

sections.title: Documentation Requirements
sections.content: Dangerous goods must be accompanied by documentation including shipper's declaration, air waybill, and emergency procedures.

sections.title: Packaging Requirements
sections.content: Dangerous goods must be packaged in accordance with specific regulations including padding, marking, and labeling requirements.

sections.title: Loading Procedures
sections.content: Dangerous goods must be loaded securely and separated from incompatible materials. Forbidden combinations must not be loaded together.

sections.title: Emergency Procedures
sections.content: In case of dangerous goods incident, crew must follow emergency procedures including use of protective equipment and communication with ground services.

sections.title: Dangerous Goods Definitions
sections.content: Dangerous goods are articles or substances which are capable of posing significant risk to health, safety or property when transported by air. Operators are not permitted to transport dangerous goods unless approved by the Authority. The operator's eligibility will be highlighted in their Air Operator's Certificate.

sections.title: Dangerous Goods Accident
sections.content: A dangerous goods accident is an occurrence associated with and related to the transport by air of dangerous goods which results in fatal or serious injury to a person or major property damage.

sections.title: Dangerous Goods Incident
sections.content: A dangerous goods incident is an occurrence other than a dangerous goods accident related to the transport by air of dangerous goods which results in injury to a person, property damage, fire, breakage, spillage, leakage of fluid or radiation or other evidence that the integrity of a package has not been maintained.

sections.title: Technical Instructions
sections.content: ICAO publishes Technical Instructions for the Safe Transport of Dangerous Goods by Air (ICAO document 9284). All operators are to take all reasonable measures to ensure dangerous goods are packed as specified in the Technical Instructions.

sections.title: Exceptions for Dangerous Goods
sections.content: Articles that would normally be classified as dangerous goods may be carried excluded from the provisions provided they are: required to be aboard for operating reasons; carried as catering or cabin service supplies; carried for use in flight as medical aid for a patient; carried by passengers or crew members.

sections.title: Labelling and Packaging
sections.content: Operators are responsible for ensuring all dangerous goods are carried and packed and labelled in accordance with the Technical Instructions. The shipper is responsible for checking that dangerous goods offered for transport are not forbidden items and are properly classified, packed, marked and labelled.

sections.title: Loading Restrictions
sections.content: Only dangerous goods specified in the Technical Instructions are to be carried in passenger cabins or on the flight deck. Where carried in cargo compartments, goods are to be loaded, segregated, stowed and secured as specified. Goods marked 'Cargo Aircraft Only' must be loaded in dedicated cargo aeroplanes.

sections.title: Provision of Information
sections.content: Operators are required to provide information to crew members about dangerous goods being carried. This includes: The nature of the goods; Emergency procedures; Location on aircraft. Crew must have access to this information throughout the flight.

sections.title: Training Requirements
sections.content: All personnel involved in the acceptance, handling and carriage of dangerous goods must receive training. This includes: Generalawareness training; Function-specific training; Safety training. Training must be documented and recurrent training provided.

sections.title: Accident and Incident Reporting
sections.content: An operator shall report dangerous goods accidents and incidents to the Authority and the appropriate Authority in the state where the accident or incident occurred. The first report shall be dispatched within 72 hours of the event and include details known at that time.

sections.title: Acceptance Checklist
sections.content: Operators are required to produce and follow a checklist (the Acceptance Checklist) for the acceptance procedure for dangerous goods. This checklist ensures all requirements are met before goods are accepted for transport.

sections.title: Inspection for Damage or Contamination
sections.content: Operators must ensure that dangerous goods are inspected for damage or contamination before loading. Damaged or leaking packages must not be accepted for transport. Contaminated packages must be properly cleaned or disposed of before transport.

sections.title: Runway Contamination
sections.content: Contaminated runways may have substances including water, snow, slush, ice, or rubber deposits that reduce friction.

sections.title: Braking Action
sections.content: Braking action is reported as Good, Medium to Good, Medium, Medium to Poor, or Poor. It provides an indication of runway surface condition.

sections.title: Surface Conditions
sections.content: Runway conditions are assessed and reported by airport operators using the runway condition code (RWYCC) system from 0 (unsafe) to 6 (dry).

sections.title: Performance Calculations
sections.content: Landing distance must be increased significantly when the runway is contaminated. The aircraft must be able to stop within the available distance.

sections.title: Hydroplaning
sections.content: Hydroplaning occurs when water separates the tires from the runway surface. It can occur at significant speeds and eliminates braking effectiveness.

sections.title: Procedures for Contaminated Runways
sections.content: Procedures include increased landing distance, adjusted approach speeds, and consideration of directional control issues.

sections.title: Contaminated Runway Definition
sections.content: A contaminated runway is one where the runway surface is not completely dry. Contamination may include: Standing water (depth more than 3mm); Snow (compact or loose); Slush (water-saturated snow); Ice (clear or rime); Wet ice; Frost; Rubber deposits. Contamination reduces friction and increases stopping distance.

sections.title: Runway Condition Code
sections.content: Runway conditions are assessed and reported using the runway condition code (RWYCC) system from 0 (unsafe) to 6 (dry). Codes 1-5 indicate various contamination conditions: 1 - Snow; 2 - Wet snow; 3 - Frost; 4 - Water; 5 - Slush; 6 - Dry.

sections.title: Braking Action Reports
sections.content: Braking action is reported as: Good - Friction coefficient 0.40 and above; Medium to Good - 0.39 to 0.36; Medium - 0.35 to 0.30; Medium to Poor - 0.29 to 0.26; Poor - 0.25 and below. It provides an indication of runway surface condition.

sections.title: Hydroplaning
sections.content: Hydroplaning occurs when water separates the tires from the runway surface. It can occur at significant speeds: At 75% of the square root of the tire pressure for dynamic hydroplaning; At lower speeds when brakes are applied. Hydroplaning eliminates braking effectiveness.

sections.title: Performance Calculations
sections.content: Landing distance must be increased significantly when the runway is contaminated. The affected landing distance depends on the type and depth of contamination. The aircraft must be able to stop within the available runway distance plus safety margin.

sections.title: Take-off on Contaminated Runways
sections.content: Take-off on contaminated runways requires: Decision speed (V1) must not be in the contaminated area; Take-off may be permitted if the contamination is not too deep; Braking may be reduced. The flight crew must consider the reduced acceleration and increased stopping distance.

sections.title: Approach Speed Adjustments
sections.content: When landing on contaminated runways, the approach speed should be increased by the gust factor as an additional margin. This provides a higher threshold speed and reduces the risk of hydroplaning.

sections.title: Directional Control Issues
sections.content: Contaminated runways may cause directional control problems: Asymmetric braking may cause directional deviations; One main gear may have less friction than the other; Crosswinds combined with contamination are particularly hazardous. The pilot must be prepared for these conditions.

sections.title: Operations on Wet Runways
sections.content: Wet runways (without contamination) are treated differently from contaminated runways. Even standing water may cause hydroplaning. The pilot should be aware of the reduced friction and adjust procedures accordingly.

sections.title: Runway Surface Assessment
sections.content: Airport operators are responsible for assessing and reporting runway conditions. Pilots should obtain the latest runway condition report before landing. Braking action reports and RWYCC codes are the primary sources of information.
